content.body: Now that we know the derivative of a function is its slope, there's a natural next question: how do we find the slope of a curve?
On a straight line, slope is constant. On a curve, the slope changes from point to point. The derivative at a point is the slope of the tangent line — the line that just grazes the curve there.

Try it: drag x along f(x) = x² and watch the tangent line graze the curve. On the left, where f is decreasing, the slope is negative; at the bottom it's flat (slope 0); on the right, where f is increasing, the slope is positive. In general, the slope is positive wherever f is increasing and negative wherever f is decreasing.
Here is f(x) = x² with its tangent line. Drag x from left to right and watch the tangent. As x increases, the tangent becomes…

options: Steeper — the slope grows
Flatter — the slope shrinks
Unchanged — the slope is constant
Steeper at first, then flatter

sandbox.caption: Drag the point on this different curve and watch the tangent tilt — read its steepness straight off the live slope value.

solution.body: Watch the tangent-slope readout as you drag x to the right along $f(x) = x^2$.
At $x = 1$ the slope is small; by $x = 3$ it's much larger. On a curve the slope isn't fixed — for $x^2$ the tangent gets steeper as x grows.

phases.text: The green tangent line shows the curve's steepness at the current x.
Drag x to the right — watch the tangent tilt up and the slope readout climb.
On a curve the slope isn't fixed: for $x^2$ the tangent keeps getting steeper as x grows.

feedback.correct: Right — on a curve the slope is not constant. For x² the tangent gets steeper as x grows.
feedback.incorrect: Watch the tangent-slope readout as you drag x to the right. Is it growing or shrinking?
feedback.hint: At x = 1 the tangent slope is small; at x = 3 it is much larger.

content.body: If the slope changes along the curve, there must be a spot where it is flat. Tap the point on f(x) = x² where the tangent is flat (slope 0).

sandbox.caption: Drag the point. The tangent goes flat right at the peak — slope 0. Now find that spot on the real curve yourself.

solution.body: The tangent is flat where the curve stops falling and starts rising — the bottom of the parabola.
For $f(x) = x^2$ that turning point is at $x = 0$, where the slope passes through 0. Tap there.

phases.text: Where is the tangent flat — slope 0? That's where the curve stops falling and starts rising.
For $f(x) = x^2$ that's the very bottom of the parabola.

feedback.correct: Exactly — at x = 0 the curve bottoms out, switching from decreasing (negative slope) to increasing (positive slope), so the slope passes through 0.
feedback.incorrect: The slope is zero where the curve is neither rising nor falling — where it stops falling and starts climbing.
feedback.hint: The lowest point of f(x) = x² is at x = 0.

content.body: How do we measure the slope at a single point? We start with something easier: the slope between two points.

A secant line is a straight line that passes through two points on the curve. Its slope is just rise over run between those points — the average rate of change of the curve from one point to the other.
Here's how that connects to calculus: keep one point fixed at x = 2 and slide the second point toward it by shrinking h. Watch what the secant slope does as the two points close in.

options: The secant slope approaches 4
The secant slope approaches 0
The secant slope stays constant
The secant slope approaches 2

sandbox.caption: Shrink h toward 0 on this different curve — the dashed secant swings onto the green tangent.

solution.body: As h shrinks, the moving point slides into the fixed one and the secant turns into the tangent at $x = 2$.
The slope of $x^2$ is $2x$, so at $x = 2$ the tangent slope is $2(2) = 4$. The secant slope closes in on 4.

phases.text: The dashed secant joins the fixed point at $x = 2$ to a second point. Its slope is their average rate of change.
Shrink the gap so $h \to 0$ — slide the second point toward $x = 2$.
The secant slope closes in on the tangent slope at $x = 2$, which is $2(2) = 4$.

feedback.correct: As the distance between the points → 0, the secant slope approaches the tangent slope at x = 2, which is 4.
feedback.incorrect: Watch the slope value as the two points get closer together.
feedback.hint: For f(x) = x², the slope at x = 2 is 2(2) = 4.

content.body: See it for yourself. The solid green line is the tangent at x = 2. Slide h down to bring the moving point toward the fixed one, and watch the dashed secant rotate until it lies right on top of the green line.
As the distance between the points → 0 and they merge into one, the secant line becomes…

options: The tangent line at that point
A horizontal line
A vertical line
A secant line through two points

sandbox.caption: Shrink h toward 0 on this different curve — the dashed secant swings onto the green tangent.

solution.body: Slide h toward 0 and the two points merge into one.
A line through two points that have collapsed to a single point is the tangent line — the dashed secant settles right onto the green tangent at $x = 2$.

phases.text: The solid green line is the tangent at $x = 2$; the dashed line is the secant through two points.
Shrink $h$ — the secant rotates toward the green tangent.
As the two points merge into one, the secant lands exactly on the tangent line.

feedback.correct: Correct — as the points merge, the secant settles right onto the green tangent line.
feedback.incorrect: Slide h toward 0 and watch which line the dashed secant lands on.
feedback.hint: The dashed secant rotates until it overlaps the green line that just grazes the curve at x = 2 — the tangent.

content.body: Here's the big picture: a curve doesn't have one fixed slope the way a straight line does. Its slope at a point is the slope of the tangent there — and you reach that tangent by shrinking a secant until its two points merge into one. Let's turn that picture into a formula.

Remember how slope works on a straight line: pick two points and take the rise over the run. Do the exact same thing on the curve. Put one point at x and a second a distance h away, at x + h. The rise is the change in the function's value between them, f(x + h) − f(x), and the run is the distance h between their inputs:
That ratio is the average rate of change over the step from x to x + h — the slope of the secant line you just explored.

The derivative does the very same thing, but at a single point. Shrink the distance h toward 0 so the second point slides into the first: the secant becomes the tangent, and the average rate closes in on the slope at that one point. That limit is the derivative:
This is the limit definition of the derivative. Now let's put it to work and pin down some specific slopes on f(x) = x².
Time to work backward. For f(x) = x², the tangent slope at x is f'(x) = 2x. Find the value of x where that slope equals 4, then drag the slider there.

interaction.goalLabel: Drag x until the tangent slope is 4

sandbox.caption: Practice the reverse move on this different curve: drag the point until the live slope reaches a value you choose, then notice where it landed.

solution.body: The tangent slope of $f(x) = x^2$ is $f'(x) = 2x$. Set it equal to the target, 4:
Drag the slider to $x = 2$, where the tangent slope is 4.

phases.text: The tangent slope of $f(x) = x^2$ is $f'(x) = 2x$.
We need slope 4, so solve $2x = 4$, giving $x = 2$ — watch the tangent rotate there.
At $x = 2$ the tangent slope is exactly 4.

feedback.correct: Nailed it — at x = 2 the tangent slope is 4. Notice the pattern: the slope of x² is 2x.
feedback.incorrect: The slope of x² is 2x. Set 2x = 4 and solve for x, then drag the slider there.
feedback.hint: Solve 2x = 4 → x = 2.

content.body: Using that pattern — the slope of x² is 2x — what is the tangent slope of f(x) = x² at x = 3?

sandbox.caption: Drag the point along this different curve and watch how the tangent's slope changes from one spot to the next.

solution.body: The slope of $f(x) = x^2$ follows the pattern $f'(x) = 2x$. Evaluate it at $x = 3$:
In general the power rule gives $f'(x) = 2x$ — the single value $f'(3) = 6$ is just this rule at $x = 3$.

phases.text: The green tangent touches $f(x) = x^2$ at $x = 3$; its slope is the derivative there.

feedback.incorrect: Plug x = 3 into the slope formula 2x.

content.body: You just found one slope, f'(3) = 6. Now generalize to the whole curve: build the derivative f'(x) of f(x) = x² with the power rule.

sandbox.caption: Step through the power rule in general form: bring the exponent down as a factor, then drop the power by one. Apply the same moves to this term.

feedback.correct: Exactly — f'(x) = 2x. The single value f'(3) = 6 is just this rule evaluated at x = 3.
feedback.incorrect: Apply the power rule to x²: bring the exponent down as the coefficient and drop the power by one.

content.body: To recap: the derivative f'(x) is the slope of the tangent line at x — the value the secant slope closes in on as the distance h shrinks to 0. For f(x) = x², that slope works out to 2x.
Working out this limit by hand for every function would be slow, though. Next, the power rule turns the derivative of a power of x into something you can write down instantly.
Here is f(x) = x². The slope readout is hidden — work out where the tangent slope equals 6, then drag the point there and check.

sandbox.caption: The slope readout is shown here. Drag the point on this different curve to feel how the tangent's slope tracks your position.

feedback.correct: Nailed it — the slope of x² is 2x, and 2x = 6 at x = 3.
feedback.incorrect: The readout is hidden, so compute it: the slope of x² is 2x. Set 2x = 6 and drag there.
feedback.hint: Solve 2x = 6 → x = 3.

content.body: The average rate of change of f(x) = x² from x = 0 to x = 2 is 2. Tap the point where the tangent slope equals that average.

sandbox.caption: Drag the point on this different curve and watch the tangent's slope — practice finding a spot with a given slope before you tap the real one.

feedback.correct: Exactly — the average rate over [0, 2] is 2, and the tangent slope 2x equals 2 at x = 1.
feedback.incorrect: Set the tangent slope 2x equal to the average rate 2 and solve for x, then tap that point.
feedback.hint: Solve 2x = 2 → x = 1, the midpoint of 0 and 2.

content.body: The fixed point sits at x = 3 on f(x) = x². Drag h to slide the second point toward it: watch the dashed secant swing onto the green tangent and the secant-slope readout settle down. As h → 0, what single value is the secant slope closing in on?

sandbox.caption: Shrink h toward 0 on this different curve — the dashed secant swings onto the green tangent.

feedback.correct: Right — as the two points merge, the secant slope closes in on the tangent slope at x = 3, which is f'(3) = 6.
feedback.incorrect: Shrink h further and read where the secant slope is heading — it never quite lands, but it's homing in on one value.
feedback.hint: The slope of x² is 2x, so at x = 3 the tangent slope is 2(3) = 6.

content.body: On y = x³ the tangent slope is 3x². Order these points from the least steep tangent to the steepest.

sandbox.caption: Drag the point and compare how steep the tangent is at different spots — the steeper it tilts, the larger the slope.

feedback.correct: Right — 3x² gives 0, 0.75, 3, and 6.75, so steepness climbs in that order.
feedback.incorrect: The slope of x³ is 3x². Compare that value at each x.
feedback.hint: 3x² gives 0, 0.75, 3, and 6.75.

content.body: The green line is the tangent at the fixed point x = 2 on f(x) = x². Drag h toward 0 and watch the dashed secant rotate. As the two points merge into one, the dashed secant settles right on top of the green line — so the secant is turning into…

options: The tangent line to the curve at x = 2
A horizontal line through the point
A vertical line through the point
Another secant through two separate points

sandbox.caption: Shrink h toward 0 on this different curve — the dashed secant swings onto the green tangent.

feedback.correct: Exactly — as h → 0 the two points become one and the secant lands on the tangent line at x = 2. Its slope is that tangent's slope, the derivative.
feedback.incorrect: Slide h all the way down and see which line the dashed secant comes to rest on.
feedback.hint: The dashed secant rotates until it overlaps the green line that just grazes the curve at x = 2 — the tangent.

content.body: The parabola f(x) = x² − 2x turns around at x = 1. Label how f behaves on each side.

options: Increasing
Decreasing

sandbox.caption: Drag the point on this different curve: where the tangent points uphill the slope is positive, where it points downhill the slope is negative.

feedback.correct: Right — f' = 2x − 2 is negative before x = 1 and positive after, so f decreases then increases.
feedback.incorrect: Check the sign of f' = 2x − 2 on each side of x = 1.
feedback.hint: At x = 0, f' = −2 (falling); at x = 2, f' = +2 (rising).

content.body: The secant slope shown here is the average rate of change of f(x) = x² from x = 1 to x = 1 + h. Drag h down from large to small and watch the readout. What happens to this average rate as the interval shrinks?

options: It closes in on the instantaneous rate — the tangent slope at x = 1
It grows larger and larger without limit
It stays exactly the same for every value of h
It drops straight to 0

sandbox.caption: Shrink h toward 0 on this different curve — the dashed secant swings onto the green tangent.

feedback.correct: Right — as the interval shrinks, the average rate of change closes in on the instantaneous rate at x = 1, the tangent slope f'(1) = 2.
feedback.incorrect: Watch the readout as h gets small. Is it blowing up, holding steady, or settling toward one value?
feedback.hint: At h = 2 the average rate is 4; by h = 0.05 it is about 2.05 — heading for 2, the tangent slope at x = 1.

content.body: For f(x) = x² − 4x + 3, the slope is 2x − 4. Tap where the tangent slope equals 2.

sandbox.caption: Drag the point and watch the slope readout change. Get a feel for reading a tangent's slope here, then find the matching spot on the real curve.

feedback.correct: Exactly — the slope is 2x − 4, and 2x − 4 = 2 gives x = 3.
feedback.incorrect: Set the slope 2x − 4 equal to 2 and solve for x, then tap that point.
feedback.hint: Solve 2x − 4 = 2 → x = 3.

content.body: The slope of the secant line between the points at x and x + h is the difference quotient below.
Reading this as rise over run, what does the denominator h stand for?

options: The run — the horizontal gap between the two inputs x and x + h
The rise — the change in the function's output
The slope of the tangent line at x
The area under the curve from x to x + h

sandbox.caption: Shrink h toward 0 on this different curve — the dashed secant swings onto the green tangent.

feedback.correct: Right — h is the run, the horizontal step from x to x + h. The numerator f(x + h) − f(x) is the rise, and rise over run is the secant's slope.
feedback.incorrect: Rise is the vertical change (the numerator). The denominator is the other half of rise over run.
feedback.hint: h sits in the denominator, so it is the run — the horizontal distance between the two x-values.

content.body: The graph shows f(x) = x² together with its slope f'(x) = 2x (the second line). A straight line has one fixed slope — but what does this picture tell you about the slope of the curve?

options: The slope changes from point to point — negative on the left, 0 at the bottom, positive on the right — so we read it off the tangent line at each point
The slope is the same everywhere, just like a straight line
The slope is 0 everywhere on the curve
The slope is positive everywhere, whatever the x-value

sandbox.caption: Drag the point along this different curve and watch the tangent — its tilt is different at every spot, even flipping from uphill to downhill.

feedback.correct: Right — the f'(x) line climbs through zero, so the curve's slope is negative on the left, flat at the bottom, and positive on the right. A curve's slope varies, and the tangent pins it down at each point.
feedback.incorrect: Look at the f'(x) line: it isn't flat. Where it sits below 0 the curve falls; where it rises above 0 the curve climbs.
feedback.hint: f'(x) = 2x passes through 0 at x = 0, so the slope goes negative, then zero, then positive — it keeps changing.

content.body: You've seen that the slope of f(x) = x² is 2x. The faint curve is f(x) = x². Drag each dot to plot the slope, 2x, at x = −2, −1, 0, 1, and 2.

sandbox.caption: Drag the point: the overlaid f′ curve shows the slope of f at each x — that height is exactly what you plot when you build a derivative graph.

feedback.correct: Exactly — the slope of x² is 2x, so the dots fall on the straight line through (−2, −4), (0, 0), and (2, 4).
feedback.incorrect: At each x the slope is 2x: that's −4 at x = −2, 0 at x = 0, and 4 at x = 2. Drag each dot to that height.
feedback.hint: Plot y = 2x: a straight line through the origin with slope 2.

content.body: The line is pinned to f(x) = x² at x = 1. Drag to rotate it until it lies tangent to the curve — until its slope matches the curve's slope there.

sandbox.caption: Drag the point on this different curve and watch the tangent — get a feel for matching a line's tilt to the curve's slope.

feedback.correct: Right — the slope of x² is 2x, so at x = 1 the tangent slope is 2.
feedback.incorrect: Rotate the line until it just grazes the curve at x = 1. The slope of x² is 2x.

content.body: You matched the tangent slope at x = 1. Now give the derivative f'(x) of f(x) = x² in general.

sandbox.caption: Step through the power rule in general form: bring the exponent down as a factor, then drop the power by one. Apply the same moves to this term.

feedback.correct: Right — f'(x) = 2x, and at x = 1 that gives the slope 2 you just matched.
feedback.incorrect: Use the power rule on x²: bring the exponent down as the coefficient and reduce the power by one.
feedback.hint: d/dx[x²] = 2x.

content.body: A phone is dropped from a ledge. Its height after t seconds is h(t) = 1.25 − 5t² (in meters), and its velocity at any time is the slope of the tangent there, h'(t).
Drag the point along the curve to t = 0.3 and read the tangent-slope readout. What is the phone's velocity at t = 0.3?

options: −5 m/s
−3 m/s
−2 m/s

sandbox.caption: Drag the point on this different curve and read the tangent-slope value — the same readout that turns a position curve into a velocity.

feedback.correct: Right — the velocity is h'(t) = −10t, so at t = 0.3 it is −10(0.3) = −3 m/s. The tangent's slope is the instantaneous velocity.
feedback.incorrect: Drag the point to t = 0.3 and read the tangent-slope readout — that slope is the velocity.
feedback.hint: At t = 0.3 the tangent-slope readout is about −3.

content.body: The same dropped phone has height h(t) = 1.25 − 5t². Its velocity at a time is the slope of the tangent there, h'(t).
Select the period when the phone's velocity is slowest.

answer.prompt: Tap the period where the curve is flattest — the smallest slope.

sandbox.caption: Drag the point on this different curve and watch where the tangent is flattest — that's where the slope is smallest in size.

feedback.correct: Right — velocity is h'(t) = −10t, smallest in size near t = 0. The curve is flattest there, so the phone moves slowest just after release.
feedback.incorrect: The slowest velocity is where the curve is flattest — where the tangent slope is smallest in size.
feedback.hint: h'(t) = −10t grows in size as t increases, so the slope is flattest at the very start.

content.body: A car's distance traveled is the faint curve. Drag to set a starting speed, press Start, then drive the speedometer to match the car's speed: read how steep the distance curve is at each moment (its slope) and steer to match.

sandbox.caption: Drag the point along this different curve and watch the slope readout — reading a curve's steepness as a rate is the move you'll use to match speed to distance.

feedback.correct: Right — speed is the slope of the distance curve, d'(t) = t. The curve starts flat (speed near 0) and steepens, so the speed climbs steadily.
feedback.incorrect: Speed is the slope of the distance curve: near 0 where the curve is flat, and larger where it climbs steeply.
feedback.hint: The distance curve starts flat — speed ≈ 0 — and gets steeper, so the speed rises steadily from there.
